content.text: Forms are the primary mechanism for collecting user input on the web. Every login screen, checkout flow, search bar, and settings panel is built on HTML form elements. Understanding the native element vocabulary — and how Angular's two form strategies map onto it — gives you a foundation for building correct, accessible, and testable input interfaces.

attrs.alt: HTML forms and input elements — form attributes, input type catalogue, label patterns, and Angular form approaches
attrs.title: HTML Forms & Input Elements

content.text: element wraps a collection of controls and defines how the browser submits them. Its three key attributes are:
Server endpoint that receives the submission. Omit for same-page or JS-handled forms.
"get" appends data as query string (bookmarkable, no side effects). "post" sends a request body (passwords, large data).
Encoding for POST bodies. Use multipart/form-data when the form includes file inputs.
Disables the browser's built-in constraint validation UI. Use when Angular handles validation instead.

<form action="/api/register" method="post" novalidate>
  <!-- controls go here -->
  <button type="submit">Register</button>
</form>
All text-like inputs share the same
attribute changes their behaviour. The browser adjusts the keyboard, native validation, and display accordingly.

<!-- Text variants -->
<input type="text"     name="name"     placeholder="Full name" />
<input type="email"    name="email"    placeholder="you@example.com" />
<input type="password" name="pwd"      placeholder="Password" />
<input type="tel"      name="phone"    placeholder="+1 555 000 1234" />
<input type="url"      name="website"  placeholder="https://example.com" />
<input type="search"   name="q"        placeholder="Search…" />

<!-- Numeric -->
<input type="number" name="qty" min="1" max="99" step="1" value="1" />
<input type="range"  name="rating" min="1" max="10" step="1" />

<!-- Date / time -->
<input type="date"           name="dob" />
<input type="datetime-local" name="appointment" />
<input type="month"          name="month" />
<input type="time"           name="startTime" />

<!-- Choice -->
<input type="checkbox" name="agree"   id="agree" />
<input type="radio"    name="plan"    id="basic"  value="basic" />
<input type="radio"    name="plan"    id="pro"    value="pro" />

<!-- Other -->
<input type="file"   name="avatar"  accept="image/*" />
<input type="color"  name="brand"   value="#3b82f6" />
<input type="hidden" name="csrf"    value="tok123" />
silently. This makes forward-compatibility safe — old browsers display a text box rather than nothing.
Key in the submitted form data. Required for the field to be included in a submission.
Links to a <label for=id>. Required for explicit label association.
Initial or current value. For checkbox/radio: the value submitted when checked.
Hint text shown when the field is empty. Not a substitute for a label.
Native validation: field must not be empty on submit.
Field is non-interactive and not included in submission.
Value is visible and included in submission but cannot be edited.
Constrain and increment the numeric or date value.
Character count limits with native validation.
Regex that the value must match (native validation).
Allows multiple file selections or comma-separated email values.
Comma-separated MIME types or extensions the picker should filter to.
Every visible form control must have a programmatically associated label. A label that is only visually near an input — with no
link or wrapping relationship — is invisible to screen readers.
Email

is not a label. Placeholder text disappears when the user starts typing, making it useless for users who need to recall what a field is asking for. Always use a visible, persistent label in addition to any placeholder.
groups related controls into a logical unit. The mandatory
element is the group's accessible name. Screen readers prepend the legend text to each control's announcement inside the fieldset, providing context for radio groups, checkboxes, and address blocks.

<fieldset>
  <legend>Delivery address</legend>

  <label for="street">Street</label>
  <input type="text" id="street" name="street" required />

  <label for="city">City</label>
  <input type="text" id="city" name="city" required />
</fieldset>

<fieldset>
  <legend>Preferred contact method</legend>
  <label><input type="radio" name="contact" value="email" /> Email</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="contact" value="phone" /> Phone</label>
</fieldset>
The form element family also includes

<!-- Dropdown -->
<label for="country">Country</label>
<select id="country" name="country">
  <option value="">-- Select --</option>
  <option value="us">United States</option>
  <option value="gb">United Kingdom</option>
  <optgroup label="Asia">
    <option value="jp">Japan</option>
    <option value="in">India</option>
  </optgroup>
</select>

<!-- Multi-line text -->
<label for="bio">Biography</label>
<textarea id="bio" name="bio" rows="5" cols="40"
          maxlength="500" placeholder="Tell us about yourself…"></textarea>

<!-- Buttons -->
<button type="submit">Submit</button>   <!-- submits the form -->
<button type="reset">Reset</button>    <!-- resets all fields to defaults -->
<button type="button">Cancel</button>  <!-- no default behaviour — attach (click) handler -->

Angular's Reactive and Template-driven forms both add
, replacing native validation with Angular's own validator pipeline.
Angular provides two complementary approaches to forms. Both ultimately bind to the same HTML elements; they differ in where the model and validation logic live.
In the component class via FormGroup/FormControl

Show error messages as text near the field, not only through colour changes. Link error text to the field with aria-describedby.
attributes on name, email, address, and payment fields. WCAG 1.3.5 requires it for inputs collecting personal data.
Ensure keyboard tab order follows the visual layout. Avoid tabindex values greater than 0.

for forms that send passwords or sensitive data. GET appends the form data to the URL, which is logged in browser history and server access logs.

on the same form. They can coexist in a project but should not both control the same form group — pick one approach per form.

Built-in validators cover most rules, but real apps need domain rules like password confirmation, unique usernames, or business date ranges. Angular lets you write synchronous and asynchronous custom validators as plain functions.
{{ usernameError }} Passwords do not match.
Async validators return an Observable or Promise and are run only after all sync validators pass, reducing unnecessary network requests.
Checking… Email already in use.
Use FormArray when the number of fields is not known at design time — an e-commerce cart with line items, a resume builder with work-experience entries, or a survey with user-added questions.
— append a new control to the array
— remove the control at the given index
— the raw array of AbstractControl; iterate in templates
— remove all controls at once (useful for resets)
Reactive forms are plain TypeScript objects, so most logic is testable without the DOM. Use TestBed only when you need to verify template bindings.
on inputs for reactive forms — setting the control directly is faster and more reliable
Test error message visibility by querying
elements after invalid values are set
Form UX and Operational Best Practices
Well-implemented form logic is necessary but not sufficient — form UX determines whether users complete and trust the form.
Show errors only after the control is
(user left the field) — avoids alarming errors on an untouched page
showError(name: string) { const c = this.form.get(name); return c?.invalid && c?.touched; }
For async validators, show a spinner while
{{ serverError() }}
clears values and resets dirty/touched/pristine flags — use after successful submission
updates only the specified controls — safe for partial updates from server
requires every control key; throws if any is missing — use for full form hydration

Template-driven forms (FormsModule + ngModel) suit simple scenarios; Reactive forms (ReactiveFormsModule + FormGroup) suit complex, dynamic, or testable forms.
